section.title: Job Replies to Zophar
section.sectionRationale: This long reply closes the first cycle of speeches. Job answers Zophar's claim to superior wisdom with biting sarcasm, then pivots to a sweeping vision of God's sovereignty over nations and kings, a fierce rebuke of his friends as worthless physicians, and finally a resolve to plead his case before God directly, whatever the cost. The unit ends with the book's first flicker of resurrection hope: if a man dies, will he live again?
section.thesis: Job matches his friends' claims to wisdom with his own, insists that God's sovereign power is plain even to animals and birds, resolves to argue his case before God though it may cost him his life, and, staring at the brevity and finality of human death, dares to ask whether a man could live again after dying.
section.frameNote: Seven movements: Job's sarcastic dismissal of the friends' claimed monopoly on wisdom; his sweeping description of God's sovereignty over nations, kings, and counselors; his rebuke of the friends as worthless physicians who would do better to stay silent; his resolve to defend his case before God though He slay him; his direct plea that God withdraw His hand and stop hiding His face; his meditation on the brevity of human life compared to a tree that can sprout again after being cut down; and his closing question, if a man dies, will he live again, followed by lament that hope, like eroding stone, wears away.

units.label: Wisdom will not die with you
units.note: ask the animals, and they will instruct you

verses.text: Then Job answered:
"Truly then you are the people with whom wisdom itself will die!

But I also have a mind; I am not inferior to you. 
Who does not know such things as these?

I am a laughingstock to my friends, though I called on God, and He answered. 
The righteous and upright man is a laughingstock.
The one at ease scorns misfortune as the fate of those whose feet are slipping.
The tents of robbers are safe, and those who provoke God are secure, those who carry their god in their hands.
But ask the animals, and they will instruct you; ask the birds of the air, and they will tell you.
Or speak to the earth, and it will teach you; let the fish of the sea inform you.
Which of all these does not know that the hand of the LORD has done this?
The life of every living thing is in His hand, as well as the {{ruach|breath}} of all mankind.
Does not the ear test words as the tongue tastes its food?
Wisdom is found with the elderly, and understanding comes with long life.

units.label: Wisdom and strength belong to God
units.note: He makes nations great and destroys them

verses.text: "Wisdom and strength belong to God; counsel and understanding are His.
What He tears down cannot be rebuilt; the man He imprisons cannot be released.
If He holds back the waters, they dry up, and if He releases them, they overwhelm the land.
True wisdom and power belong to Him. The deceived and the deceiver are His.
He leads counselors away barefoot and makes fools of judges.
He loosens the bonds placed by kings and fastens a belt around their waists.
He leads priests away barefoot and overthrows the established.
He deprives the trusted of speech and takes away the discernment of elders.
He pours out contempt on nobles and disarms the mighty.
He reveals the deep things of darkness and brings deep shadows into light.
He makes nations great and destroys them; He enlarges nations, then disperses them.
He deprives the earth's leaders of reason and makes them wander in a trackless wasteland.
They grope in the darkness without light; He makes them stagger like drunkards.

units.label: Worthless physicians
units.note: if only you would remain silent

verses.text: "Indeed, my eyes have seen all this; my ears have heard and understood.
What you know, I also know; I am not inferior to you.
Yet I desire to speak to the Almighty and argue my case before God.
You, however, smear with lies; you are all worthless physicians.
If only you would remain silent; for that would be your wisdom!
Hear now my argument, and listen to the plea of my lips.
Will you speak wickedly on God's behalf or speak deceitfully for Him?
Would you show Him partiality or argue in His defense?
Would it be well when He examined you? Could you deceive Him like a man?
Surely He would rebuke you if you secretly showed partiality.
Would His majesty not terrify you? Would the dread of Him not fall upon you?
Your maxims are proverbs of ashes; your defenses are defenses of clay.

units.label: Though He slay me, I will hope in Him
units.note: I will still defend my ways to His face

verses.text: "Be silent, and I will speak. Then let come to me what may.
Why do I put myself at risk and take my life in my own hands?
Though He slay me, I will hope in Him. I will still defend my ways to His face.
Moreover, this will be my salvation, for no godless man can appear before Him.
Listen carefully to my words; let my declaration ring in your ears.
Behold, now that I have prepared my case, I know that I will be vindicated.
Can anyone indict me? If so, I will be silent and die.

units.label: Why do You hide Your face

verses.text: "Only grant these two things to me, so that I need not hide from You:
Withdraw Your hand from me, and do not let Your terror frighten me.
Then call me, and I will answer, or let me speak, and You can reply.
How many are my iniquities and sins? Reveal to me my transgression and sin.
Why do You {{sathar|hide}} Your face and consider me as Your enemy?
Would You frighten a windblown leaf? Would You chase after dry chaff?
For You record bitter accusations against me and bequeath to me the iniquities of my youth.
You put my feet in the stocks and stand watch over all my paths; You set a limit for the soles of my feet.
So man wastes away like something rotten, like a moth-eaten garment.

units.label: Man is short of days and full of trouble
units.note: there is hope for a tree

verses.text: "Man, who is born of woman, is short of days and full of trouble.
Like a flower, he comes forth, then withers away; like a fleeting shadow, he does not endure.
Do You open Your eyes to one like this? Will You bring him into judgment before You?
Who can bring out clean from unclean? No one!
Since his days are determined and the number of his months is with You, and since You have set limits that he cannot exceed,
look away from him and let him rest, so he can enjoy his day as a hired hand.

For there is hope for a tree: If it is cut down, it will {{tsemach|sprout}} again, 
and its tender shoots will not fail.
If its roots grow old in the ground and its stump dies in the soil,
at the scent of water it will bud and put forth twigs like a sapling.
But a man dies and is laid low; he breathes his last, and where is he?
As water disappears from the sea and a river becomes parched and dry,
so a man lies down and does not rise. Until the heavens are no more, he will not be awakened or roused from sleep.

units.label: If a man dies, will he live again?
units.note: so You destroy a man's hope

verses.text: "If only You would hide me in Sheol and conceal me until Your anger has passed! If only You would appoint a time for me and then remember me!

When a man dies, will he live again? All the days of my hard service I will wait, 
until my {{chaliphah|renewal}} comes.
You will call, and I will answer; You will desire the work of Your hands.
For then You would count my steps, but would not keep track of my sin.
My transgression would be sealed in a bag, and You would cover over my iniquity.
But as a mountain erodes and crumbles and a rock is dislodged from its place,

as water wears away the stones and torrents wash away the soil, 
so You destroy a man's hope.
You forever overpower him, and he passes on; You change his countenance and send him away.
If his sons receive honor, he does not know it; if they are brought low, he is unaware.
He feels only the pain of his own body and mourns only for himself."

ruach.gloss: Breath, wind, spirit, the animating life-force God gives to every living thing. Job argues that even the animals know the hand of the LORD holds the ruach of all mankind, a truth so basic it needs no elaborate philosophy, only observation of the created world.

sathar.gloss: To hide, conceal. Job's anguished question, why do You hide Your face, becomes one of the most repeated laments in the Psalms; here it names the specific pain of feeling treated as an enemy by a God who will not show Himself or explain.

tsemach.gloss: A sprout or shoot, new growth from what appeared finished. Job observes that even a felled tree can sprout again at the scent of water, a hope explicitly denied to human beings in this chapter, which sharpens rather than answers his coming question about life after death.

chaliphah.gloss: A renewal, relief, or change, from a root meaning to pass on or be replaced. Job's hope that his chaliphah will come, after his hard service is finished, is one of the boldest and most fragile hopes in the entire book, reaching toward the possibility of life after death without yet having any certainty of it.

basic.context: Job answers Zophar's claim to superior wisdom with sharp sarcasm, insisting he knows everything the friends know, and that even the animals and birds understand that the LORD's hand governs all things. He then paints a sweeping picture of God's total sovereignty over nations, kings, counselors, and priests, before turning to rebuke his friends bluntly as worthless physicians who would do better to stay silent. He resolves to argue his case before God directly, even at the risk of death, uttering one of the boldest statements of faith in the book, though He slay me, I will hope in Him. He then meditates on the brevity of human life, noting that even a felled tree can sprout again but a dead man does not rise, and dares to ask the question the whole book has been building toward: if a man dies, will he live again?
basic.hermeneutics: This speech shows Job's faith deepening even as his theology remains unresolved. His declaration of hope even if God slays him is not naive optimism but a bold trust that persists without any promise of rescue. His question about life after death is not answered here, and should not be forced into a fuller resurrection doctrine than the text itself claims; it is a genuine, aching question, a seed that Scripture's later revelation will bring to full flower.
basic.originalLanguages: Job argues that even creatures know the ruach, breath, of all mankind rests in God's hand. He asks why God will sathar, hide, His face and treat him as an enemy, a lament that becomes a fixture of the Psalms. He observes that a tree can tsemach, sprout, again after being cut down, but longs, without certainty, for his own chaliphah, renewal, after death, one of the most poignant unanswered hopes in the Old Testament.
basic.typology: Job's question, if a man dies, will he live again, plants a seed that will not fully bloom until the New Testament's clear revelation of bodily resurrection in Christ. His declaration, though He slay me, I will hope in Him, anticipates the kind of trust that holds even when God's actions look like abandonment, a trust perfected in Christ's own submission in Gethsemane.
basic.christ: Job's trembling question about life after death finds its full and certain answer in the resurrection of Jesus Christ, the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, proof that the hope Job could barely dare to voice is in fact secured. Where Job could only wish for a time appointed and a remembering, the gospel announces the day fixed and the Redeemer who has already risen, guaranteeing that all united to Him will rise as well.
basic.god: God is described here in totalizing sovereign terms, tearing down what cannot be rebuilt, humbling kings and counselors, yet Job experiences that same sovereignty as terrifying rather than comforting, since it currently seems turned against him. The tension between God's greatness and God's apparent hostility remains unresolved, driving Job's daring resolve to plead his case directly rather than to give up.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 15:20-22 answers Job's question directly, Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection of the dead; Psalm 13:1 echoes Job's lament, how long will You hide Your face from me; Habakkuk 3:17-18 models a similarly bold trust, though the fig tree does not blossom, yet I will rejoice in the LORD.
basic.oneStory: Job's fragile, daring question, if a man dies, will he live again, is one of the great unresolved hopes of the Old Testament, and the whole of Scripture moves to answer it fully in the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. His declared trust, though He slay me, I will hope in Him, models the kind of faith that holds on even without an answer, until the answer finally comes.

context.literary: This long speech closes the book's first cycle of dialogue, moving through sarcasm, cosmic praise of God's sovereignty, direct rebuke of the friends, bold legal resolve, and finally a meditation on death that reaches its highest point yet in the book's unfolding hope.
context.historical: The extended catalog of God overturning kings, counselors, and priests, 12:17-25, reflects genuine historical experience of dynasties rising and falling, a common ancient Near Eastern observation that Job repurposes to argue for God's total and sometimes bewildering sovereignty.
context.canonical: Job 14:14's question, if a man dies, will he live again, stands among the Old Testament's earliest gestures toward resurrection hope, later developed more explicitly in passages like Daniel 12:2 and Isaiah 26:19, before its full revelation in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Job matching his friends' wisdom claim for claim while deepening his resolve to plead his case before God directly, and to voice, without resolving, the question of life after death that the rest of Scripture will answer.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes Job's bold resolve and his tentative hope of renewal after death; it does not prescribe a fully developed doctrine of resurrection, which awaits later, fuller revelation. What does endure as prescriptive truth is the legitimacy of trusting God even under threat of death, and of bringing life's hardest questions directly to Him rather than suppressing them.

debates.question: How much does Job 14:14 actually teach about resurrection?

views.label: A genuine, if undeveloped, hope of bodily resurrection
views.case: The question, if a man dies, will he live again, paired with the hope of a chaliphah, renewal, after his hard service, points toward some form of life beyond death that the rest of the chapter otherwise denies is possible.

views.label: A rhetorical question expressing longing rather than settled doctrine
views.case: The surrounding verses, 14:10-12, seem to deny any return from death in strong terms; Job may be voicing an aching wish rather than an actual conviction, especially since he does not return to this hope with full confidence until chapter 19.

debates.bearing: Either way, this verse functions as a genuine seed of hope that Scripture's later, fuller revelation of the resurrection in Christ brings to complete clarity; readers should neither overstate Job's certainty here nor dismiss the passage's real theological significance.

study.originalLanguages: Job argues even creatures know that the ruach, breath, of all mankind rests in God's hand, and later asks why God will sathar, hide, His face, treating him as an enemy. He observes a felled tree can tsemach, sprout, again, yet a man does not rise the same way, though he still dares to hope for his own chaliphah, renewal or relief, after death, a hope Scripture's later revelation will confirm and complete.

typology.type: Job's fragile hope of a chaliphah, renewal, after death
typology.fulfillment: 1 Corinthians 15:20-22, Christ the firstfruits of the resurrection, guaranteeing the renewal Job could only tentatively hope for.

typology.type: Job's resolve, though He slay me, I will hope in Him
typology.fulfillment: Christ's own submission in Gethsemane, not my will but Yours, trusting the Father even unto death.

study.christ: Job's trembling question, if a man dies, will he live again, finds its full and certain answer only in the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ, described by Paul as the firstfruits of all who will rise. Where Job could only long for an appointed time and a God who remembers him, the New Testament announces that the appointed day has come in history, in an empty tomb, guaranteeing that the renewal Job barely dared to hope for is secured for everyone united to Christ by faith.
study.god: God is described in sweeping, total sovereignty, over nations, kings, counselors, and priests, a picture Job does not dispute but currently experiences as hostile rather than comforting, since that same sovereign hand seems turned against him. The unresolved tension between God's greatness and God's apparent distance drives Job to resolve, boldly, to plead his case directly rather than fall silent.

crossReferences.note: Christ the firstfruits of those who have fallen asleep, directly answering Job's question of whether a man who dies will live again.
How long will You hide Your face from me, echoing Job's lament in 13:24 as a recognized, legitimate form of prayer.
Though the fig tree does not blossom, yet I will rejoice in the LORD, a kindred bold trust held without present evidence of rescue.

study.oneStory: Job's climactic question in this speech, if a man dies, will he live again, is one of the boldest and most fragile hopes in the Old Testament, planted here without full resolution. The whole of Scripture answers it decisively in the resurrection of Jesus Christ, and Job's declared trust even under threat of death, though He slay me, I will hope in Him, models the faith that holds on until that answer comes.

sermon.title: If a Man Dies, Shall He Live Again?
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly on Job 13:20-24 and 14:7-17, the exact heart of this passage, drawing out the comfort of God's presence, the challenge of His glory, and the joy of the resurrection as the resources Job reaches for even without full answers.

quiz.q: How does Job answer Zophar's implicit claim to superior wisdom in 12:1-3?

options.text: With sarcasm, insisting he is not inferior to them and that even the animals and birds understand the truths they claim as special insight.
options.note: Job's opening mocks the friends' assumption that wisdom belongs uniquely to them, verses 2-3 and 7-9.

options.text: By declaring plainly that he has a mind of his own and knows exactly what they know.
options.note: A true part of Job's response, though less complete than his sharper sarcastic appeal to the animals and birds.

options.text: By apologizing for his earlier complaints.
options.note: Job does not retract his earlier laments; he intensifies his case and his resolve to bring it before God.

options.text: By admitting his friends know far more than he does.
options.note: Job explicitly claims equality with them, not inferiority.

quiz.q: What bold statement of trust does Job make in 13:15?

options.text: Though He slay me, I will hope in Him, and I will still defend my ways to His face.
options.note: This is one of the most quoted verses in the book, expressing trust that persists even under threat of death.

options.text: He resolves to keep speaking and let come to him what may, since no godless man can appear before God anyway.
options.note: A true and closely related resolve from the same passage, though less precise than the exact wording of trusting God even if slain.

options.text: I am certain God will never harm me further.
options.note: Job's statement assumes the real possibility of being slain; it is trust despite danger, not a claim of guaranteed safety.

options.text: If He slays me, I will finally curse Him as my wife suggested.
options.note: This is the opposite of Job's actual declaration; he affirms continued hope rather than cursing God.

quiz.q: What contrast does Job draw between a tree and a man in 14:7-12?

options.text: A felled tree can sprout again at the scent of water, but a man who dies does not rise in the same way.
options.note: This contrast sharpens rather than resolves Job's coming question about whether a man could live again after death.

options.text: A tree has real hope of new growth even after being cut down, while a man who dies simply lies down and does not rise.
options.note: A close restatement of the same contrast, though slightly less precise than the exact detail about the tree budding at the scent of water.

options.text: Neither trees nor men have any hope of renewal.
options.note: The tree explicitly does have hope of sprouting again; the man's case is presented as the harder, contrasting one.

options.text: Both trees and men are guaranteed to be restored after death.
options.note: The passage explicitly denies this for man, which is exactly what makes Job's question in verse 14 so pointed.

quiz.q: What question does Job ask in 14:14, and how does Scripture ultimately answer it?

options.text: If a man dies, will he live again; the New Testament answers this fully in the bodily resurrection of Christ, the firstfruits of all who will rise.
options.note: 1 Corinthians 15:20-22 presents Christ's resurrection as the guarantee of the very hope Job could only tentatively voice.

options.text: Whether there is any renewal after his hard service on earth is finished, a hope Scripture later confirms in Christ's resurrection.
options.note: Correctly points toward Job's chaliphah, renewal, and its answer in Christ, though less exact than the precise question of verse 14 itself.

options.text: A question Scripture never actually answers.
options.note: The New Testament answers this question with unambiguous clarity in the doctrine of bodily resurrection secured by Christ.

options.text: Whether Job's children will be restored to him in this life.
options.note: The question in 14:14 concerns life after death in general, not the specific restoration of his own children.

quiz.q: What rebuke does Job give his friends in 13:4-5?

options.text: That they smear with lies and are worthless physicians, and that remaining silent would actually be their wisdom.
options.note: This is one of Job's sharpest direct rebukes of his friends' counsel in the entire book.

options.text: That their maxims are proverbs of ashes and their defenses are defenses of clay.
options.note: A true and vivid rebuke from the same speech, though less direct than Job's explicit charge that they smear with lies.

options.text: That they have not suffered enough to understand him.
options.note: Job's specific complaint here is about the falseness and uselessness of their arguments, not primarily about their lack of personal suffering.

options.text: That they should speak even more than they already have.
options.note: Job says the opposite, that silence would have been wiser than what they have said.

questions: Job insists that even animals and birds know basic truths about God's sovereignty that his friends claim as special wisdom. Where have you seen simple, obvious truths get lost under layers of clever argument, in yourself or others?
Job declares, though He slay me, I will hope in Him. What would it look like for you to keep trusting God even in a situation where the outcome looks like it could go very badly?
Job asks God directly to reveal his sin rather than assume it, and to stop hiding His face. Where do you need to bring a direct, honest question to God rather than settling for silence or assumption?

Job's question, if a man dies, will he live again, is answered decisively in Christ's resurrection. How does the certainty of resurrection change the way you face your own mortality or grieve the death of someone you love?

Job calls his friends worthless physicians who would have done better to stay silent. When you comfort someone in pain, how do you discern when to speak and when silence would truly be the wiser gift?

translationNotes.note: Breath renders ruach, the same word used of the Spirit of God elsewhere in Scripture; here it names the life-force God gives and sustains in every living creature.

This verse is textually debated in the Hebrew manuscript tradition, with some ancient witnesses reading 'I have no hope' instead of 'I will hope in Him'; the BSB, like most modern translations, follows the reading of confident trust, which fits the flow of Job's continued resolve to argue his case before God in the following verses.

Renewal renders chaliphah, from a root meaning to pass on or be replaced; the word carries a sense of relief or exchange, Job hoping for a change from his current hard service to something new after death, without yet full certainty of what that change would be.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture toolkit for Job 12:1-14:22. The em dash in verse 12:6 of the source text was replaced with a comma per house style; no other substantive changes were made to the verse text.
